metadata.title: Why a Metabolic Enzyme Mutation Can Lock Cells Into an Immature State
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Biology candidate 07. Source chapter: cancer-biology/chapters/07-epigenetics-in-cancer-the-methylation-and-histone-code.md (IDH mutation / 2HG / demethylase competitive inhibition / ivosidenib differentiation therapy only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = demethylase active / differentiation possible / healthy state; CRIMSON #BF3339 = 2HG / blocked / silenced / blast-locked; GOLD = editor's-pen highlight used once; never swapped mid-film
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO full TCA cycle chemistry, NO TET1/2/3 isoforms, NO IDH-mutant glioma vs AML distinction, NO histone demethylase mechanism detail, NO DNMT inhibitor comparison. One mechanism only: 2HG is a structural mimic of alpha-ketoglutarate that competitively poisons demethylases, locking differentiation genes silent; ivosidenib removes 2HG and the blasts mature into neutrophils.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B15: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. A bone marrow biopsy. The marrow is packed with blasts — immature, arrested cells that cannot grow up. The diagnosis: acute myeloid leukemia.

card.copy: Why a metabolic enzyme mutation can lock cells into an immature state
card.sub: IDH mutation, 2-hydroxyglutarate, and the epigenetic block

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE CARD] eyebrow CANCER BIOLOGY, title + sub

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE CARD] eyebrow CANCER BIOLOGY, title + sub

beats.narration_text: The patient's sequencing report comes back. One amino acid change in a metabolic enzyme called IDH1. That enzyme normally runs a step in the citric acid cycle. Nothing to do with cell division. Nothing to do with growing up. And yet somehow, that single amino acid change froze the entire leukemic population in an immature state.

props.headline: The patient's sequencing report comes back. One amino acid change in…

beats.scene_description: a bone marrow biopsy histology image — dense clusters of immature blast cells with large nuclei, few mature cells visible; slow push-in on the packed blast population; desaturated newsprint treatment
beats.image_prompt: B02, bone marrow biopsy histology slide, acute myeloid leukemia blasts, dense population of immature round cells with prominent nuclei, few mature segmented cells, clinical pathology image, desaturated flat print reproduction, editorial collage style on aged newsprint
beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] packed AML bone marrow — the blast arrest

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [PHOTO] packed AML bone marrow — the blast arrest

beats.narration_text: A metabolic enzyme mutation should change how the cell extracts energy from nutrients — not lock cells into an immature state. Here a single amino acid change in IDH1 causes cancer. Why does a metabolic enzyme mutation produce an epigenetic block to differentiation?

card.copy: A metabolic enzyme mutation should change energy production — not freeze development. Why does a single amino acid change in IDH1 lock cells in an immature, cancer-prone state?

beats.new_visual_element: [QUESTION CARD] the film's one question on screen

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [QUESTION CARD] the film's one question on screen

beats.narration_text: The naive picture is straightforward. Cells differentiate by reading developmental programs — genes that switch on in sequence as a blast matures into a functional neutrophil. The cell's metabolic machinery and its developmental program seem to be completely separate systems. Change a metabolic enzyme and you'd expect to change metabolism. Full stop.

production_viz.label: metabolism and differentiation — two separate systems
production_viz.mechanic: two distinct boxes side by side: left labeled 'METABOLIC ENZYMES' (slate), right labeled 'DIFFERENTIATION GENES' (teal); a gap between them with a label 'no connection expected'
production_viz.note: establish the naive expectation — these systems seem decoupled

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two boxes — metabolism vs differentiation — no connection

beats.narration_text: But the genome's gene expression machinery needs cofactors to work. Methyl marks that silence genes are written and erased by enzymes — and those enzymes depend on small molecules from metabolism to run. The metabolic state sets the cofactor supply. And cofactor supply shapes the epigenome.

production_viz.label: metabolites are cofactors for epigenetic enzymes
production_viz.mechanic: the gap between the two boxes from B04 now fills with a connecting arrow labeled 'cofactors'; a small molecule glyph (circle) flows from the metabolic box to the epigenetic machinery box
production_viz.note: the connection is revealed — metabolism feeds the epigenetic eraser enzymes

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] cofactor arrow connects metabolism to epigenetic erasers

beats.narration_text: Here is what IDH1 normally does. It converts isocitrate to alpha-ketoglutarate — a metabolite that the demethylase enzymes need to remove methyl marks from DNA and histones. When methyl marks silence a differentiation gene, the demethylases use alpha-ketoglutarate to strip the mark and restore transcription.

production_viz.label: normal IDH: isocitrate to alpha-KG, demethylases erase marks
production_viz.mechanic: IDH enzyme (slate box) takes isocitrate in, outputs alpha-KG (teal circle) with arrow to demethylase; demethylase removes methyl mark from a simplified DNA/histone glyph (methyl mark crimson dot disappears); differentiation gene activates (teal)
production_viz.note: show the normal pathway — alpha-KG feeds the demethylase which erases silencing marks

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] normal IDH → alpha-KG → demethylase erases methyl → gene ON

beats.narration_text: The mutant IDH1 enzyme carries one amino acid change in its active site. That one change corrupts its chemistry. Instead of making alpha-ketoglutarate, the mutant enzyme runs a different reaction and makes a structurally similar molecule called 2-hydroxyglutarate — an oncometabolite that the cell does not normally produce.

production_viz.label: mutant IDH: alpha-KG diverted to 2HG
production_viz.mechanic: the IDH box morphs — gains a crimson mutation marker; the output arrow that led to alpha-KG (teal) is crossed out; a new crimson output arrow produces 2HG (crimson circle, labeled '2-hydroxyglutarate'); the 2HG floods the right side of the frame
production_viz.note: the manim move: morph — the enzyme changes and so does its product

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] mutant IDH morphs — alpha-KG output replaced by 2HG flood

beats.narration_text: 2-hydroxyglutarate is the structural mimic. Its shape is close enough to alpha-ketoglutarate that it fits into the demethylase active site — the same pocket where alpha-KG would normally bind and power the reaction. But 2HG cannot complete the reaction. It sits in the active site like a key that fits the lock but does not turn it, blocking the real key from entering.

production_viz.label: 2HG sits in the demethylase active site — competitive block
production_viz.mechanic: demethylase shown as an outlined keyhole shape; first, a teal key (alpha-KG) fits and turns, methyl mark is erased; then scene resets — a crimson key (2HG, same shape) sits in the keyhole and does not turn, blocks the teal key; methyl mark persists on the gene glyph
production_viz.note: the core mechanism — structural mimic that fits but cannot complete the reaction; the GOLD highlighter can mark the keyhole to show the point of competition

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] key-in-lock: 2HG (crimson) blocks alpha-KG (teal) from the demethylase

beats.narration_text: With the demethylases competitively poisoned, methyl marks accumulate across the genome — including on the promoters of the very genes that direct blast differentiation. Those genes go silent. The blasts cannot read their own maturation instructions. They are stuck.

production_viz.label: methyl marks pile up on differentiation gene promoters
production_viz.mechanic: a row of gene promoter glyphs (simplified rectangles) initially with no marks; crimson methyl dots accumulate one by one on each promoter; a teal 'differentiation gene' label fades to crimson strikethrough 'SILENCED'; blasts pile up as crimson circles
production_viz.note: show accumulation — the methylation builds because erasers are blocked

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] methyl dots accumulate on promoters, differentiation genes silenced

beats.narration_text: IDH mutations appear in roughly ten percent of acute myeloid leukemia cases and in most low-grade gliomas. In the leukemia, the result is a marrow overrun by blasts that pile up without ever maturing. It is a cancer of arrested development — cells that are immature not because they are proliferating too fast, but because they cannot read the next instruction.

props.headline: IDH mutations appear in roughly ten percent of acute myeloid leukemia…

beats.scene_description: a simplified schematic of normal blood cell development — a stem cell at top branching down through progressively more mature cells toward a segmented neutrophil at the bottom; the blast-stage node is circled in crimson, arrest arrow pointing to it; below that branch is empty
beats.image_prompt: B10, hematopoiesis diagram, stem cell at top branching to mature blood cells, blast stage circled in red indicating arrest, segmented neutrophil at bottom, simplified educational diagram style, editorial flat illustration, desaturated newsprint treatment
beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] hematopoiesis diagram — blast arrest circled, maturation blocked

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [PHOTO] hematopoiesis diagram — blast arrest circled, maturation blocked

beats.narration_text: Consider one IDH1-mutant AML patient starting ivosidenib — a drug that blocks the mutant IDH1 enzyme itself. Day zero: 2HG is one thousand times normal, demethylases are ninety percent inhibited, blasts are clogging the marrow. These numbers are illustrative.

production_viz.label: Day 0 — 2HG 1000x, demethylases 90% off, marrow packed
production_viz.mechanic: three state cards in a row: '2HG: 1000x' (crimson), 'DEMETHYLASES: 10% active' (crimson), 'BLASTS: high' (crimson); all three read alarming; label 'Day 0' above
production_viz.note: illustrative numbers; establish the starting state — everything in the red zone

beats.narration_text: Day fourteen: ivosidenib has blocked the mutant enzyme. 2HG has halved. The demethylases are recovering. Day twenty-eight: 2HG is near normal, the demethylases are active again, and on the blood smear the blasts are maturing — acquiring the segmented nuclei of functional neutrophils. The cancer resolves not because the cells died, but because they finally grew up.

production_viz.label: Day 14 and Day 28 — recovery arc
production_viz.mechanic: the three state cards animate forward: Day 14 column appears — 2HG halved (moving from crimson toward teal), demethylases recovering (partial teal); Day 28 column — 2HG near normal (teal), demethylases active (teal), blasts maturing (teal); the rightmost column glows teal
production_viz.note: show the recovery arc — ivosidenib drops 2HG, demethylases reactivate, blasts differentiate

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] Day 14 and 28 columns — crimson to teal recovery arc

beats.narration_text: A metabolic enzyme mutation changes energy metabolism — and, through the metabolite it floods the cell with, also poisons the molecular erasers that clear silencing marks from differentiation genes. The answer to the question: 2-hydroxyglutarate is a structural mimic of the cofactor the demethylases need. It fits their active site but blocks the reaction, locking methyl marks in place and locking the cell in an immature state.

card.copy: 2HG mimics alpha-KG — fits the demethylase but blocks it, locking methyl marks that silence differentiation genes.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [ENDCARD] question → answer, CANCER BIOLOGY kicker
